---
title: "R projects and working directories"
author: "Nicky Wakim"
title-slide-attributes:
    data-background-color: "#3070BF"
format: 
  revealjs:
    theme: "../simple_NW.scss"
    chalkboard: true
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
    width: 1955
    height: 1100
    footer: "R projects and working directories"
    html-math-method: mathjax
    highlight-style: ayu
code-annotations: hover
execute:
  echo: true
  freeze: auto
---

## What is a Working Directory?

The **working directory** is the folder R is currently "looking at" — the starting point for all file paths in your session.

```r
# See your current working directory
getwd()
#> [1] "/Users/jane/Documents/my_project"

# Any relative path starts from here
read.csv("data/raw/survey.csv")
# R looks for: /Users/jane/Documents/my_project/data/raw/survey.csv
```

. . .

::: {.callout-warning}
If R can't find a file, the working directory is almost always the culprit.
:::

---

## The Problem: Setting it Manually is Fragile

You *can* set the working directory by hand — but you shouldn't.

```r
# Breaks on any other computer
setwd("/Users/jane/Desktop/my_project")

# Also breaks if you move the folder
setwd("C:/Users/jane/Documents/school/BIOL401/my_project")
```

. . .

This is exactly the absolute path problem from last lesson.  
**R Projects solve this automatically.**

---

## What is an R Project?

An **R Project** is a file (`.Rproj`) that sits in your project folder and tells RStudio:

> *"This folder is the project root. Set the working directory here automatically."*

::: {.large-code}
```
my_project/
├── my_project.Rproj   ← the R Project file
├── data/
├── scripts/
└── outputs/
```
:::

When you open the `.Rproj` file, RStudio sets the working directory to that folder — every time, on any computer.

---

## Creating an R Project

In RStudio: **File → New Project → New Directory → New Project**

![](https://support.posit.co/hc/en-us/article_attachments/360003990994)

. . .

Give the project a name, choose where to save it, and click **Create Project**.  
That's it — RStudio drops a `.Rproj` file in the folder.

::: {.callout-tip}
You can also create a project from an **existing folder**: **File → New Project → Existing Directory**
:::

---

## Opening and Using an R Project

**Always open your project by clicking the `.Rproj` file**, not by opening scripts directly.

```r
# Once your project is open, this just works:
read.csv("data/raw/survey.csv")

# No setwd() needed. Ever.
```

. . .

RStudio shows the active project name in the **top-right corner**. If it says *"Project: (None)"*, you're not in a project.

::: {.callout-warning}
Opening a script directly (by double-clicking it) does **not** activate the project or set the working directory.
:::

---

## The `here` Package (Bonus Tool)

For scripts in subfolders, use `here::here()` to build paths from the project root — even more robustly than relative paths alone.

```r
library(here)

# Always starts from the project root, regardless of
# where the script lives
read.csv(here("data", "raw", "survey.csv"))
write.csv(cleaned, here("data", "processed", "survey_clean.csv"))
```

. . .

::: {.callout-tip}
`here()` is especially useful in **Quarto/R Markdown** documents, where the working directory can behave unexpectedly.
:::

---

## Summary

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**The workflow:**

1. Create **one R Project** per analysis
2. Open work by clicking the **`.Rproj` file**
3. Use **relative paths** — no `setwd()`, ever
4. Optionally use **`here::here()`** for extra robustness
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
R Projects + good folder structure from last lesson = analysis that runs reliably anywhere, for anyone.
:::
:::

. . .

**Up next:** Reading data into R


## Creating project in RStudio

-   Way to designate a working directory: basically your home base when working in R

    -   We have to tell R exactly where we are in our folders and where to find other things

    -   A project makes it easier to tell R where we are

-   Basic steps to create a project

    -   Go into RStudio

    -   Create new project for this class (under `File` or top right corner)
        
        - I would chose "Existing Directory" since we have already set up our folders
        - Make the new project in the `EPI_525_F24` folder

-   Once we have projects, we can open one and R will automatically know that its location is the start of our working directory

## The nice thing about R projects

- 5 minute video explaining some of the nice features of R projects

<https://rfortherestofus.com/2022/10/rstudio-projects>


## Reproducibility

- Research data and code can reach the same results regardless of who is running the code

  - This can also refer to future or past you!
  
- We want to set up our work so the entire folder can be moved around and work in its new location

## Nicky's process for R projects

-   Let me show you my process

    -   I will create one in my Sample_folder

    -   I will show you how I switch between classes
    
- Make a project file in your class folder!

```{r}
#| echo: false
countdown::countdown(5)
```
